posts.text:
Can 350+ years of political philosophy help us govern AI? 🤔

Our new paper explores how social contract theories apply to algorithmic decision-making in welfare, policing, and hiring.

Published in AIES 2025 👇

https://doi.org/10.1609/aies.v8i1.36575 🧵

The challenge: AI systems now make decisions that affect millions, but they weren't designed with democratic legitimacy in mind.

Transparency gaps, algorithmic bias, and global reach stretch traditional theories of political authority. 🤖

We examine how Hobbes, Locke, Rousseau, Rawls, and Nozick's frameworks handle three key questions:

• Who's accountable for algorithmic mistakes?
• Can people meaningfully consent to AI governance?
• How do we measure fairness in opaque systems? ⚖️

Classical social contract theories offer valuable insights but weren't built for algorithmic governance.

Our analysis reveals where these 350-year-old frameworks need updating for AI-driven decision-making. 📜

The stakes are real: AI systems increasingly determine who gets welfare benefits, police attention, job interviews, and public services.

We need governance frameworks that can handle both human and algorithmic authority. 🏛️

Results suggest hybrid approaches may be needed—combining procedural safeguards, participatory elements, and fairness principles adapted for algorithmic contexts.

No single theory handles all the challenges AI governance presents. 🔄

Curious what you think—does this match what you're seeing in AI governance challenges?

Paper: https://doi.org/10.1609/aies.v8i1.36575

#AIGovernance #ResponsibleAI

[Optional: tag journal/outlet here]

linkedin.text:
Can centuries-old political philosophy help us govern AI? Our new research published in AIES 2025 explores this question: https://doi.org/10.1609/aies.v8i1.36575

🤖 AI systems now make critical decisions in welfare distribution, policing, hiring, and public policy. But here's the challenge: these systems weren't designed with democratic legitimacy principles in mind.

📜 We examined how social contract theories from Hobbes, Locke, Rousseau, Rawls, and Nozick apply to algorithmic governance, focusing on three key areas:

✅ Accountability: Who owns responsibility for algorithmic mistakes?
✅ Consent: Can people meaningfully opt out of AI governance?
✅ Fairness: How do we measure justice in opaque, data-driven systems?

➤ Our analysis reveals that while classical theories offer valuable insights, they weren't built for the unique challenges of algorithmic authority—transparency gaps, bias, and global reach.

➤ Results suggest hybrid approaches may be needed, combining procedural safeguards with participatory elements and fairness principles adapted for AI contexts.

• No single theoretical framework handles all the governance challenges that AI systems present
• Policy practitioners need systematic approaches to legitimacy in algorithmic decision-making
• The stakes are real: AI increasingly determines access to benefits, services, and opportunities

For governance teams and policy practitioners working on AI accountability, this framework offers a structured way to think about legitimacy in algorithmic systems. [Add coauthors] [Add funder]

GRAIL Center | Purdue University

#AIGovernance #ResponsibleAI #AIEthics #TechPolicy #PublicPolicy #AlgorithmicAccountability

[Optional: tag journal/outlet here]
